data.title: Devon Energy: Creating Value With Low Oil Prices
data.description:
I am rating Devon Energy (NYSE:DVN) with a Buy and a $40 price target. Based on historical valuations of a 4.8x EV/EBITDA multiple, the company is undervalued, trading at roughly 4.1x. Before diving into the main points of this article, I will provide a summary of Devon Energy, then proceed to answer a key question that addresses the rationale for my rating and price target: Can Devon still generate value in a low oil price environment?

Company Description

Founded in 1971, Devon Energy is an independent E&P (exploration and production) company producing oil, natural gas, and natural gas liquids. Based in the United States, the company has assets in several key onshore oil and gas basins.

Asset Profile

According to Devon’s 2024 SEC Form 10-K, the Delaware Basin is located in the Permian Basin, spanning New Mexico and West Texas. It is Devon’s largest asset in terms of production and reserves. The Delaware basin is approximately 63% of Devon’s production and 58% of its proved reserves. As of the end of 2024, Delaware produced 465 MBoe/d, consisting of 47% oil, with “413 gross wells drilled”. The company targets the “Wolfcamp, Bone Spring, Avalon, and Delaware formations,” oil and liquids-rich within this basin. For 2025, Devon has allocated 55% of its CAPEX toward the Delaware basin.

Devon’s Rockies assets comprise the Williston Basin and the Powder River Basin. The Rockies account for 15% of Devon’s production, or 107 MBoe/d with an oil profile of 61%, and 22% of its proved reserves. Situated between eastern Montana and western North Dakota, the Williston Basin formation consists of the Bakken and Three Fork formations, which are oil-rich, high-margin properties. The Powder River Basin in Wyoming is an emerging light-oil play focused on the “Turner, Parkman, Teapot, and Niobrara formations.”

Next is the Eagle Ford Basin, which spans Dewitt and Karnes counties in south Texas. This is an oil-rich play with solid margins due to low-cost and pricing at Gulf Coast prices. The Eagle Ford produces 75 MBoe/d, which is 11% of the company’s production, yielding 58% of its output in oil. Proved reserve represents 6% of the total company reserves.

Still in development, the Anadarko Basin in Oklahoma has provided Devon 82 MBoe/d or 11% of its production. This play is under a joint venture development with Dow.

Balance Sheet and Liquidity

As of March 31, 2025, Devon has cash, cash equivalents, and restricted cash of $1.23 billion. Total liquidity at the end of the second quarter of 2025 was $4.2 billion, comprised of $3 billion revolving Senior Credit Facility, which has no outstanding borrowings against it, and a cash balance of $1.23 billion. Under its credit agreement, Devon must maintain a funded debt-to-total capitalization ratio of no more than 65%. As of March 31, 2025, Devon has a debt-to-capitalization ratio of 26.3% and is in compliance with its covenant.

Total debt at the end of the first quarter of 2025 was approximately $8.88 billion, including $485 million due on December 15, 2025. Its next maturity is in 2026, with roughly $1 billion. To fund the Grayson Mill acquisition, Devon issued two Senior Notes totaling $2.24 billion in August 2024. The first tranche was a $1.25 billion senior note due 2024 at 5.25% interest. The second tranche was a 5.75% $1 billion senior note maturing in 2054. In addition, in September 2024, Devon took a Term Loan of $1 billion to help fund the Grayson Mill acquisition.

Creating Value

Devon is a great company. However, it's still in the commodity business, subject to price volatility. While the company cannot control oil prices, it can control its expenses and capital spending to maintain or improve profitability in a weak oil price environment.

The wrong thing producers could do is boost production in a falling oil price outlook. This strategy may help to maintain cash flow in the short run, but it destroys shareholder value in the long run. Instead, Devon has chosen to reduce costs, focus on profitability, and rely on “capital discipline rather than growing production in a saturated oil market,” thus preserving long-term value.

In April 2025, the company announced its “Business Optimization Plan,” designed to “improve margins and capital efficiency.” Devon implemented a $1 billion cost-saving program spanning capital efficiency, production improvement, renegotiation of contractual midstream commitments, and corporate overhead. Approximately $400 million of the cost savings is expected to occur in 2025 and should impact 2026 financial results. Management aims to complete its optimization plans by the end of 2026, which is expected to increase free cash flow by $1 billion in 2027.

In addition, Devon cut 2025 CAPEX by $100 million to $3.7-$3.9 billion, which is not expected to impact production. Management’s plans include reducing the number of rigs in the Delaware Basin from 14 to 11 in the second half of 2025 while improving its completion rate and drill spend to help maintain production levels.

Cash Flow Expectation

During its first quarter 2025 earnings call, management provided financial guidance for 2025. Management’s forecast highlights the company’s ability to generate cash flow in a weak price environment. For 2025, Devon expects oil production to range from 382 to 388 million barrels per day. Over 25% of Devon’s 2025 oil and natural gas production is hedged, thus providing downside protection should oil prices further deteriorate. The company estimates a break-even oil price of $45 per barrel, which includes a fixed dividend payment.

The company provided a sensitivity analysis of three possible scenarios to highlight its ability to generate free cash flow at various oil prices. In the worst case, the company estimates it can generate $1.9 billion in free cash flow with a $50 WTI price. The base case is $2.6 billion with $60 WTI, and the best-case scenario is $3.3 billion at $70 WTI.

Return to Shareholders

Devon generates strong cash flow, which is used to return cash to shareholders through cash dividend payouts and stock repurchases. The company’s dividend comprises a fixed dividend, which is tied to cash flow, and a variable dividend linked to high commodity prices.

On November 4, 2024, Devon did not declare a variable dividend due to “recent pullback in commodity prices and equities, as well as the additional leverage taken.” Instead, the company will return cash to shareholders through share repurchases and its fixed cash dividend payment.

In the first quarter of 2025, the company paid a fixed dividend of $.24 per share for $163 million. Compared to the first quarter of 2024, the fixed dividend increased by $.02 per share. For the second quarter of 2025, Devon announced it will pay a fixed cash dividend of $154 million, or $.24 per share. Assuming Devon will continue to pay a quarterly cash dividend payment of $.24 per share ($.92 annually), the company has a 2.84% yield.

Also, during the first quarter of 2025, the company repurchased $301 million in common shares. As of the end of March 2025, the company has repurchased $3.6 billion or 77.5 million common shares since the Board of Directors authorized it a $5 billion stock repurchase program.

Non-Quantifiable Value

Devon has had a long history as an independent E&P. Managing the commodity cycle for nearly six decades in an industry where its peers were either acquired or went bankrupt, one could argue that Devon is one of the last few independent oil companies left, still delivering value to shareholders. Consistency is a value hard to quantify in a model or multiple assumptions; nevertheless, it should be noted.

Financial Analysis

For 2025, I estimate Devon can generate revenues of $16.2 billion, an increase of 1.87% over the 2024 actual of $15.9 billion. The revenue increase is due to the incremental production from the Grayson Mill acquisition and its full-year impact.

Overall, my forecast is driven by a lower oil price assumption. The model assumes a $60 WTI spot price and a $3.50 natural gas price. Other key assumptions include 25% of oil and natural gas hedged at $68 barrels and $3.25 natural gas. Resulting in a realized oil price estimate for 2025 of $62.88 per barrel, compared to the 2024 actual realized price of $73.78 per barrel. The cost-saving initiative implemented by management of $400 billion by the end of 2025 does not have a material impact on financial results until 2026.

My 2025 EBITDA estimate for Devon is $6.95 billion, lower than the 2024 results by 9.6%. The company reports EBITDAX, which adds back exploration expenses. Other companies do not use this non-GAAP metric. For ease of comparison, I adjusted from EBITDAX to EBITDA by excluding exploration expenses.

Devon Energy - Valuation

My price target for Devon is $40 per share based on my 2025 EBITDA estimate of approximately $7 billion, using an EV/EBITDA multiple of 4.8x and assuming an average price of $62 per barrel of oil for 2025. The stock is currently trading at 4.1x EV/EBITDA.

Below is a sensitivity analysis based on my model. The table estimates, under various scenarios, the price targets at different oil prices and multiple assumptions. The row lists the various oil price assumptions. The vertical column assumes the current trading EV/EBITDA multiple. Based on the sensitivity analysis, Devon’s stock trades at $31 per share, reflecting a $60 oil price assumption, with an EV/EBITDA multiple of 4.1x. My target price is based on average realized oil price of $62 per barrel with a 4.8x multiple.

Historical EV/ EBITDA Multiple

Since 2020, Devon's average EV/EBITDA multiple has been 4.8x, as shown in the table below. These calculations are based on the company’s average annual stock price and financial results. At first, the 4.8x multiplier may seem high, given what the company is currently trading, but it’s a fair assumption on a historical basis. Also, I am giving management the benefit of the doubt as to whether it can manage the balance sheet with its debt load and continue to return capital to shareholders at its current pace.

Devon is trading near the lower end of the group compared to its peers at a forward 3.9x EV/EBITDA basis, as shown in the table below from Seeking Alpha’s comps. The lower trading multiple is due to the company's electing not to declare its variable dividend due to the debt incurred of $3.25 billion to fund the Grayson Mill acquisition and a weak commodity price outlook. As of the first quarter of 2025, the acquisition increased its total debt to roughly $8.8 billion.

Risks to my Thesis

OPEC+ is on its way to restoring 2.2 million barrels per day of production. Despite adding roughly 1.3 million barrels of new output since April 2025, oil prices have remained resistant due to solid consumer demand and tight inventory balances. However, if demand weakens from a weak consumer or the impact of trade negotiations, oil prices may fall further on the demand side.

A further reduction in oil prices is the main risk in my Buy rating on Devon, affecting its ability to generate free cash flow, repurchase stock, make dividend payments, and execute its $2.5 billion debt reduction program. Thus, increasing the probability of Devon suspending its stock buyback and suspending or reducing its cash dividend payments.

In addition, Devon's debt incurred from the Mills acquisitions is a significant concern. Loading up the balance sheet with an additional $3.25 billion in debt adds further risk to Devon’s financial flexibility.

Summary

In conclusion, I am commending coverage of Devon Energy with a Buy rating and a $40 price target. Management has implemented a cost-saving plan to offset weaker oil prices. These goals create value and allow the company to maintain free cash flow and return capital to shareholders in a weak commodity price environment.

data.title: Arm Holdings: Not Good Enough
data.description:
Ever since going public last year, Arm Holdings plc (NASDAQ:ARM) has traded as if the market didn't really understand the royalty payment system. The chip platform isn't benefitting from the AI GPU surge like the stock trades. My investment thesis remains ultra-Bearish on the stock, especially after this rally back up to $140.

Relatively Small

Arm Holdings is definitely building the future of computing, but the company was responsible for building the past of computing and wireless. Most investors don't understand over 310 billion Arm-based chips have been shipped since inception, with 30.6 billion shipped last year.

The company only generates about $0.13 in revenue per each Arm-based chip shipped each year. The company produced just $4 billion in revenues last year and only $2 billion was related to royalty revenues, making the average royalty rate at just $0.065 each.

The AI race is currently being developed via a few million AI GPUs from Nvidia (NVDA) generating close to $200 billion in annual sales now, not via a boost in the smartphone and computer chips using designs from Arm. If anything, the AI PC boom hasn't occurred and companies like Apple (AAPL) are struggling to sell additional iPhones.

On the FQ4'25 earnings call, CEO Rene Haas talked about the AI chip designs using Arm technology as follows:

Arm is now increasingly the first choice for AI cloud deployments. We expect up to 50% of new server chips at hyperscalers to be Arm-based this year.

Arm NVIDIA's Grace Blackwell, Armv9 is now in full production. Google's Axion Armv9 now deployed in 10 regions used by 40 of their top 100 customers, offering up to 65% better price performance than current-generation x86. And Microsoft Cobalt 100 supports major workloads for Databricks, Siemens, Snowflake and internal services like Teams and Copilot. And over 50% of new AWS CPU capacity in the past two years is powered by Arm-based Graviton.

We're also seeing strong momentum in custom silicon with companies turning to Arm for CPU, GPU and NPU solutions. This is driving both license and royalty growth. NVIDIA's AI desktop, DGX Spark powered by the Grace Blackwell superchip with Armv9 CPUs is gaining traction, reinforcing strong demand for Arm based AI infrastructure.

Arm reported a booming FQ4 due to fluctuations in high-value license agreements after the prior 2 quarters saw revenue slip, while the excitement about the above AI chips is only a few million chips. The company has more consistent royalty revenue growth due to much higher royalty rates of modern chip architectures.

The chip company generates most of the revenue growth via higher license and royalty rates for the newest chip architecture. Armv9 royalty rates are double the prior generation Armv8 rates, and the business is only about 30% based off Armv9 tech.

The company only saw chip shipments grow 2% in the quarter, though smartphone royalties grew 30% YoY due to the higher rates.

Weak Start

Arm Holdings guided to FQ1 revenues of only $1.05 billion. The consensus analyst estimates forecast the chip company produces 13% growth, though management guided to 25% to 30% royalty sales growth.

Even with the slower growth, Arm still only plans $625 million in operating expenses. Arm Holdings will produce a quarterly profit per share of up to $0.40, which begs the question of whether the company will actually warrant higher royalty rates considering the high margins.

Qualcomm (QCOM) won the recent court case filed by Arm in another sign the chip company will struggle to raise royalty rates outside of new technology. Qualcomm won the case, allowing the wireless company to pay the lower royalty rates versus the rates paid by its acquisition Nuvia.

The stock already has a market cap of $150 billion. Arm Holdings trades at 30x FY26 sales targets of $4.8 billion and nearly 80x the EPS target of $1.78. The consensus analyst estimates have earnings only growing 9% this year, with sales hitting an annual growth trajectory of ~20% over the next 3+ years.

Takeaway

The key investor takeaway is that Arm Holdings appears heavily involved in the AI chip race, but the company just doesn't benefit due to the limited chips used in AI data centers. Since the business is only benefiting from higher royalty rates, the growth boost won't last as more and more chip designs are moved to the v9 architecture and the company sees less of a boost from the tech shift.

Investors should continue selling Arm Holdings on all rips.
